context: Current closing price of GE Aerospace (GE): $284.60 USD as of 2026-04-24.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a modest upward drift across time horizons, anchored to what I would have assessed as the prevailing price level near 285–287 as a baseline. At the one-day horizon, I expected very little directional movement, so the estimate of 286.5 essentially assumes mean reversion and continuation of recent price action. At one week, the slight uptick to 288 reflects a small positive drift assumption consistent with longer-run equity market tendencies and the absence of any immediately obvious catalyst for a sharp decline. At one month, the estimate of 292 continues that mild upward trend, incorporating the general historical tendency for broad equity indices or assets priced in this range to drift higher over multi-week windows, while remaining humble about the magnitude of any move.

The confidence interval widths grow substantially as the horizon extends, which is the standard consequence of uncertainty compounding over time. At one day, the 90% interval of roughly plus or minus 14.5 points reflects typical intraday-to-overnight volatility for an asset in this price range. At one week, the interval widens to roughly plus or minus 26 points, incorporating the possibility of macro data surprises, central bank communications, or geopolitical headlines that could shift sentiment meaningfully. At one month, the interval of roughly plus or minus 47 points captures the full range of scenarios that can unfold over four weeks, including earnings seasons, economic data revisions, and shifts in risk appetite.

Specific risks that could push outcomes outside these intervals include a sudden escalation in geopolitical conflict, an unexpected central bank policy pivot — either a surprise rate hike or emergency cut — or a major financial institution experiencing stress that triggers broader contagion. On the upside, a stronger-than-expected resolution to ongoing trade or diplomatic tensions, or a surprisingly robust economic data release, could push prices well above the upper bound of the one-month interval. Liquidity events, such as forced deleveraging by large funds or a flash crash triggered by algorithmic feedback loops, represent tail risks that are difficult to quantify but could temporarily breach even the wide one-month bounds.

It is also worth noting that these intervals were constructed under relatively normal market conditions and may understate true tail risk. Historical volatility can shift rapidly, and if realized volatility were to spike — as it does during stress episodes — the actual distribution of outcomes would have fatter tails than my stated intervals imply. Forecasters consistently underestimate how often markets produce moves that appear to lie in the extreme tails of a normal distribution, so readers should treat the outer bounds of these intervals as soft guardrails rather than hard limits.
